title: AB 83, the “Stop Foreign Money in California Elections Act” passed out of the Elections Committee (5-2) and is now headed to the Appropriations Committee. The bill prohibits foreig

claims.claim_text: The bill prohibits foreign influenced corporations from contributing to candidates, parties, or committees to protect the integrity of California’s self-governance.
Federal law does not address the issue of political spending by U.S. corporations that are partially owned by foreign investors.
Foreign investments in US companies have increased significantly in recent years.
In 1982, foreign investors owned about five percent of all U.S. corporate equity.
By 2019, foreign investors owned 40% of U.S. equity, including foreign governments.
Governments throughout the world own substantial positions in politically active U.S. companies.
Norges Bank has a stake in more than 9,000 companies worldwide and holds an average of 1.4% of the U.S.’s publicly traded companies.
Saudi Arabia plans to invest about $10 billion into companies, more than doubling its assets by 2025.
Elon Musk became the largest shareholder of Twitter after acquiring the company.
Prince Alwaleed bin Talal bin Abdulaziz of Saudi Arabia became Twitter’s second largest shareholder after Musk, owning approximately 4% of the company.
The US Supreme Court’s 2010 ruling in Citizens United v. FEC created a loophole that allows foreign interests to influence corporate political activity.
AB 83 aims to close the loophole established by Citizens United and ensure California elections are free from foreign influence.
A corporation would be deemed 'foreign influenced' if 1% of shares are owned by a single foreign investor.
A corporation would be deemed 'foreign influenced' if 5% of shares are owned by multiple foreign investors.
A corporation would be deemed 'foreign influenced' if a foreign entity participates in decision-making regarding state or local political spending.
